video_title: Iran Ratchets Up Talk of Controlling Hormuz; Stocks Rally | Horizons Middle East & Africa 6/30/2026
gist: On the last trading day of a quarter that is global equities' best in ~6 years (Asia's best in 17), the show weighs Iran's push to assert toll-based control over the Strait of Hormuz against surprisingly resilient supply chains, a 40-year-low yen on intervention watch, and an AI-led rotation where incumbents like Microsoft (worst month since 2000) are being reframed as tech's value stocks.

themes.theme: Hormuz control, tolling and shipping risk

insights.claim: Iran is pushing for total dominance over the Strait of Hormuz with the ultimate aim of extracting revenue by tolling all shipping that transits, and says it will do so with or without Oman's help.
insights.speaker: Stewart Livingstone Wallace
insights.so_what: A toll regime would raise structural transit costs for global tanker traffic and set a contested legal precedent.
insights.quote: the aim ultimately is to extract some huge source of revenue from all the shipping that goes through there

insights.claim: Iran has threatened full, swift action against any vessel using the so-called southern route hugging Oman's coast, effectively forcing traffic to acknowledge its control of the waterway.
insights.speaker: Stewart Livingstone Wallace
insights.so_what: Route restrictions narrow safe transit options and raise the odds of a fresh maritime incident.
insights.quote: they are will take full swift action if anyone uses the so-called southern route, the hugs the coast of Oman

insights.claim: Under law-of-the-sea transit passage, international waterways like Hormuz are toll-free; Iran can only lawfully charge specific service fees such as navigation or pilotage, so any toll regime would break precedent and require explicit published terms.
insights.speaker: Victoria Mitchell
insights.so_what: A tolling claim is legally vulnerable, meaning enforcement rather than legitimacy would drive shipping behavior.
insights.quote: under the, the law, the sea transit passage is not told... the precedent is for a toll free passage through international waterways like the Strait of Hormuz

insights.claim: Shipping through Hormuz will remain fragmented and cautious because each operator makes individual corporate risk-tolerance decisions, and the loss of the traditional central channel forces use of northern and southern corridors with different capacity and different counterparties (Iran vs Oman).
insights.speaker: Victoria Mitchell
insights.so_what: No single 'reopening' signal exists; transit normalization will be uneven and slow.
insights.quote: everyone's making their own individual corporate decisions about the risk tolerance and the way that they approach shipping through these waterways

insights.claim: Operational disruption in Hormuz will persist for months because the assumption of mines in the central channel will take a long time to resolve, keeping the traditional route unusable.
insights.speaker: Victoria Mitchell
insights.so_what: Even a diplomatic thaw would not quickly restore full central-channel capacity.
insights.quote: things like the assumption of mines in the central channel of the strait, for example, are going to take a lot longer to resolve

insights.claim: Morgan Stanley warns of an oil surplus as Hormuz traffic rebounds, even as Iran insists on maintaining control of the strait.
insights.speaker: anchor (headline)
insights.so_what: A projected surplus caps oil upside despite the geopolitical overhang, consistent with Brent trading ~$72 and about 1% lower.
insights.quote: Morgan Stanley warns of an oil surplus as Hormuz traffic rebounds

insights.claim: Washington and Tehran gave conflicting accounts of Doha talks: Iran confirmed sending a delegation but says it will not negotiate directly with the US, working instead through Qatari and other mediators.
insights.speaker: Stewart Livingstone Wallace
insights.so_what: Indirect format signals continued friction but not collapse, keeping geopolitical risk in a holding pattern.
insights.quote: they are sending a delegation to Doha, but it looks like they're probably not going to talk to the American delegation directly

insights.claim: The earlier direct Switzerland meeting was the anomaly, not the norm; mediated talks are standard, so a return to indirect negotiation should not be read as a breakdown.
insights.speaker: Stewart Livingstone Wallace
insights.so_what: Investors overreacting to 'no direct talks' headlines may be mispricing the actual trajectory.
insights.quote: Switzerland in some respects was an anomaly in this entire process and that they spoke directly to each other

insights.claim: Global equities are on track for their best quarter in almost six years, driven by piling into perceived AI-buildout beneficiaries.
insights.speaker: anchor
insights.so_what: Momentum into quarter-end concentrates risk in AI names ahead of jobs data and the Doha talks.
insights.quote: global stocks are on track for their best quarter in almost six years

insights.claim: Asian equities are set for their best quarter in 17 years, led by South Korea's KOSPI (best since 1998, up over 100% year-to-date), Taiwan (best since 2001, up 3% on the day) and Japan's Nikkei (record 38% quarterly gain).
insights.speaker: Winnie Su
insights.so_what: The AI trade is a regional-index-level event in Asia, not just a US mega-cap story.
insights.quote: MSCI Asia here ahead it for its best quarter in 17 years

insights.claim: Over the past quarter Samsung gained about 98% and SK Hynix about 225%, the two biggest KOSPI constituents, but there is now rotation into Taiwanese hardware names away from Korean memory chips.
insights.speaker: anchor / Winnie Su
insights.so_what: Leadership within the AI-hardware trade is rotating, a signal to watch for froth in Korean memory names.
insights.quote: Samsung gain about 98%, and S.K. Hynix get about 225%

insights.claim: Hong Kong shares are sliding for a third straight quarter specifically due to their lack of AI exposure, isolating them from the regional rally.
insights.speaker: Winnie Su
insights.so_what: AI exposure, not China proximity, is the differentiating factor for Asian equity performance.
insights.quote: Hong Kong shares continue to struggle for a third quarter due to the lack of AI exposure

insights.claim: Large incumbent tech companies like Microsoft are now the 'value stocks' of tech and a low-risk way to play AI, even though Microsoft is heading for its worst month since 2000 with an ~18% June drop.
insights.speaker: Haig Bathgate
insights.so_what: Reframes the Microsoft selloff as an entry point rather than a warning, favoring incumbents over IPOs.
insights.quote: we now start right now, starting to think that these sort of large incumbent tech companies are the value stocks of, you know, of tech

insights.claim: The biggest AI valuation risk is the forthcoming IPOs of OpenAI and Anthropic, because their monetization model is unproven (Google-style ads/subscription vs Anthropic's business focus) and only 2-3 players will survive.
insights.speaker: Haig Bathgate
insights.so_what: Positions the coming AI IPO wave as the main downside catalyst for the whole AI trade.
insights.quote: the biggest AI risk is is definitely around the forthcoming IPOs

insights.claim: SpaceX is fundamentally different from OpenAI/Anthropic because it has real revenues and a real business, unlike the pure-play AI labs at the epicenter of hype, with a side narrative about a possible Tesla merger.
insights.speaker: Haig Bathgate
insights.so_what: Differentiates upcoming issuers by revenue quality, cautioning against lumping SpaceX with AI labs.
insights.quote: Space actually has proper real revenues... they have a much more real business than maybe anthropic or OpenAI do

insights.claim: IPO price signals are unreliable because free floats are small and lock-ups distort the true market, as seen in a euphoric start followed by collapse.
insights.speaker: Haig Bathgate
insights.so_what: Warns investors not to read newly-listed AI names' prices as fair value.
insights.quote: it's a very small free float... some investors have lock ups and so on as well. So we're not really seeing a true market

insights.claim: Asian AI-linked incumbents such as Tencent trade at sub-10 price-earnings ratios ahead of near-bumper earnings, making them cheap relative to US names and a likely destination for capital moving out of frothy AI trades.
insights.speaker: Haig Bathgate
insights.so_what: Flags a valuation gap that could drive rotation into cheap Asian tech.
insights.quote: companies like $0.10 I mean they're sub ten price earnings ratios

insights.claim: Markets are not overly complacent on Iran because the Russia-Ukraine war showed supply chains adjust fast, and the Hormuz squeeze has been far less significant than feared, limiting Iran's leverage window.
insights.speaker: Haig Bathgate
insights.so_what: Argues the geopolitical risk premium in equities is justifiably small given adaptive supply chains.
insights.quote: What we do know from the Ukrainian Russian war is that supply chains can adjust pretty fast

insights.claim: Strong quarter-over-quarter equity numbers are partly a base effect and rebound from the weak quarter when the war started, not purely fresh fundamental strength.
insights.speaker: Haig Bathgate
insights.so_what: Cautions that the headline 'best quarter' figure is flattered by an easy comparison base.
insights.quote: we're seeing good quarter and quarter numbers at the moment because of base effect

themes.theme: Yen at 40-year low and intervention watch

insights.claim: The yen has slid past 162 to a 40-year low (weakest since 1986), with traders now eyeing 164-165, yet no intervention has occurred because the slow, grinding pace of weakness keeps authorities on the sidelines despite talk of bold action.
insights.speaker: Winnie Su
insights.so_what: The orderly pace, not the level, is what suppresses intervention risk near-term.
insights.quote: given how slow the pace has been and just the yen grinding lower, the risk of intervention for now is still looking a bit less

insights.claim: Strong demand at Japan's two-year bond auction suggests investors expect the Bank of Japan to move more slowly and steadily after its recent rate hike.
insights.speaker: Winnie Su
insights.so_what: A dovish-paced BOJ keeps rate-differential pressure on the yen intact.
insights.quote: pretty strong demand for the two year bond auction... an indication of investors potentially seeing the Bank of Japan to move more slowly

insights.claim: Gold is trading around $4,000 an ounce and, with little on the docket this week to relieve dollar pressure, the 4000 gold and 160-plus yen levels remain in play.
insights.speaker: anchor
insights.so_what: Persistent dollar strength and record gold reflect a durable macro backdrop this week.
insights.quote: the 4000 level in gold are still in play

themes.theme: Fed path and US jobs data

insights.claim: Futures point to one Fed rate increase before year-end, but with midterms looming and a vocal president on monetary policy, the Fed is expected to stay data-driven and avoid preemptive moves, acting only if Thursday's payrolls come in strong.
insights.speaker: Haig Bathgate
insights.so_what: A strong jobs print is the trigger for a hike; anything else means the Fed holds.
insights.quote: the futures market are pointing to one increase before the end of the year

insights.claim: The US Supreme Court rejected Trump's bid to fire Fed Governor Lisa Cook without proof of wrongdoing in a split ruling, while separately expanding presidential power to fire top officials by overturning a 91-year-old precedent.
insights.speaker: anchor
insights.so_what: Fed independence is partially protected for now, but the broader removal-power ruling raises longer-term institutional risk.
insights.quote: The US Supreme Court has rejected President Trump's bid to fire Federal Reserve Governor Lisa Cook without proof of wrongdoing in a split ruling

insights.claim: China's June PMIs stayed just above water (manufacturing 50.3, non-manufacturing 50.2) but mask a two-speed economy where booming tech-heavy exports (semiconductors, robots) offset property-driven weakness in consumption and investment.
insights.speaker: Erica Tay
insights.so_what: Exports are the only growth engine, leaving China vulnerable if trade slows.
insights.quote: export really, being the only game in town

insights.claim: China is launching a 7 trillion RMB 'six networks' push to build next-generation infrastructure (5G-advanced, 6G, data centers, electricity grid upgrade) to grow computing power and set up five years of high-productivity growth.
insights.speaker: Erica Tay
insights.so_what: A state-directed digital/energy infrastructure buildout is China's designated next growth engine as property fades.
insights.quote: a new push worth 7 trillion, RMB called the six networks push

insights.claim: The Middle East conflict vindicated China's self-sufficiency drive, accelerating diversification away from seaborne energy imports, ramping EV usage to cut oil/gas demand, and expanding clean energy that needs a next-gen grid to move power from remote western/northern regions to the demand-heavy east and south.
insights.speaker: Erica Tay
insights.so_what: Structural Chinese energy diversification is a persistent demand headwind for seaborne oil and gas.
insights.quote: China trying to diversify away from seaborne shipments... ramping up its usage of, electric vehicles. So that has greatly reduced its need, for, oil and gas

insights.claim: Dubai's DFM General Index gained about 11% in the April-June quarter (its best in a year), erasing 2026 losses, with the key signal being resilience through a genuine systemic shock (oil-export plunge, aviation stoppage, missile attacks) rather than the size of the rebound.
insights.speaker: Srini Sivabalan
insights.so_what: Dubai proved it can absorb a crisis without capital flight, strengthening its long-term investment case.
insights.quote: this is a demonstration of resilience... Dubai's resilience in the case of a systemic shock

insights.claim: The Dubai rally has broadened beyond the old real-estate-and-banks hype narrative into laggards like Air Arabia and Dubai Taxi, signaling more substantial broad-based economic growth and demand for new listings and themes.
insights.speaker: Srini Sivabalan
insights.so_what: Broadening leadership implies a healthier, more durable rally than the prior 300% real-estate-led run.
insights.quote: the current rally is no longer about, the previous hype narratives we heard about real estate and banks

insights.claim: Taiwan authorities raided Supermicro offices in an investigation into alleged smuggling of Nvidia chips into China, and Supermicro shares ended the US session more than 8% lower.
insights.speaker: anchor
insights.so_what: Escalating chip-smuggling enforcement is a fresh regulatory risk for AI hardware supply-chain names.
insights.quote: authorities in Taiwan have rated the offices of Supermicro as part of an investigation into the alleged smuggling of Nvidia chips into China

insights.claim: Eni and Adnoc's investment arm (XRG) agreed to each acquire 32% of three upstream blocks in Argentina's Vaca Muerta basin as part of an integrated LNG development, with YPF retaining 36%.
insights.speaker: anchor
insights.so_what: Gulf and Italian capital is expanding into Argentine LNG, a notable cross-border energy supply diversification move.
insights.quote: Italian energy giant any and Adnoc investment arm have agreed to buy stakes in a liquefied natural gas production and export project in Argentina

insights.claim: South Africa faces nationwide anti-migrant demonstrations, and the real investor concern is not immigration policy but instability in economic-engine regions like Gauteng and KwaZulu-Natal, with the government spending an estimated $37 million on security and the country's investment story at risk.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Zabasajja
insights.so_what: Perceived government inability to control unrest could deter long-term investment despite it being framed as an economic-frustration story amid 30%+ unemployment.
insights.quote: the broader risk is South Africa's investment story

insights.claim: UK Labour leadership contender Andy Burnham's devolution-heavy pitch risks worrying markets if he changes fiscal rules to a ten-year window and is tempted into short-term giveaways to juice the economy before a 2029 election, though the UK being the most fiscally centralized in the G7 gives the agenda some merit.
insights.speaker: Lizzie Burton
insights.so_what: A potential Burnham premiership introduces UK fiscal-rule and short-termism risk that markets may eventually price.
insights.quote: that could worry markets
